title: 第 44 節
en: The Vedic poets no longer glorify the sky or the dawn, they do not celebrate the prowess of Indra, or the wisdom of Visvakarman and Prae/apati. They move about, as they say themselves, 'as if enveloped in mist and idle speech V Another says 2 : ' My ears vanish, my eyes vanish, and the light also which dwells in my heart ; my mind with its far-off longing leaves me ; what shall I say, and what shall I think ?'

Or again, ' Knowing nothing myself, I ask the seers here, who know ; ignorant myself, that I may learn ; Pie who established the six worlds, is he that One which exists in the form of the unborn Being 3 ? '
These are the storms that announce a brighter sky, and a new spring.
1 Rig- Veda, X, 82, 7, niharewa prawtta^ f/alpya ka asutrzpaA uktliasasa/t &aranti.
2 Ibid., VI, 9, 6, vi me karwa patayata7<, vi £akshuA vi idam gyotih Im'daye ahitam yat ; vi me mana7i A-arati duraadhiA kim svit vakshyatni kim u nu manishye.
3 Ibid., I, 164, 6, aA-ikitvan &ikitusha7t kit atra kavin prikkJisOm. vidmane na vidvan vi ya/t tastambha sha£ ima ra^amsi aj/asya rape kim api svit ekam.

At last1, the existence of that One, the Self, is boldly asserted, as existing by itself, existing before all created things, existing so long before the gods, that even they, the gods, do not know, from whence this creation sprang.

' Before there was anything,' we are told, ' before there was either death or immortality, before there was any distinction between day and night, there was that One. It breathed breathless by itself. Other than it there nothing since has been. There was darkness then, everything in the beginning was hidden in gloom — all was like the ocean, without a light. Then that germ which was covered by the husk, the One, was brought forth by the power of heat.' So the poet goes on brooding on the problem of the beginning of all things, how the One became many, how the unknown became known or named, how the infinite became finite ; and he finally breaks off with these lines : ' Who knows the secret 1 who proclaimed it here ? Whence, whence this manifold creation sprang 1 The gods themselves came later into being — Who knows from whence this great creation sprang ? He from whom all this great creation came,— Whether his will created or was mute,— The most high seer, that is in highest heaven, He knows it, or perchance even he knows not/ These ideas which in the hymns of the Kig-Veda appear only like the first dim stars, become more numerous, and more brilliant as time goes on, till at last they form a perfect galaxy in what is called the Upanishads, the last literary compositions which still 1 Rig- Veda, X, 129, 2.
PHILOSOPHY AND RELIGION. 317 belong to the Vedic period, but which extend their influence far beyond its limits.
The philosophy of the TTpanishads.
You remember that, next to what we call the age of the hymns, followed the age of the Brahmawas, ancient prose works intended to describe and to illustrate the ancient sacrifices.
At the end of the Brahina?ias we generally find what is called an Aranyaka, a forest book, a book intended for those who have left their house to dwell in the solitude of the forest.

And at the end of the Aranyakas again or incor porated within them, we find the oldest Upanishads, literally Sessions, or assemblies of pupils round their master ; and in those Upanishads all the religious philosophy of the Vedic age is gathered up.

In order to give you an idea of the wealth of thought collected in these Upanishads, I may tell you that it was at first my intention to devote the whole of these lectures to an exposition of the doctrines of the Upanishads. I should have found ample material in them ; while now I can only give you the slightest sketch of them in the short time that is still left to me.

There is not what could be called a philosophical system in these Upanishads. They are, in the true sense of the word, guesses at truth, frequently con tradicting each other, yet all tending in one direction. The key-note of the old Upanishads is ' Know thy Self,' but with a much deeper meaning than that of the TviaOi a-eavrov of the Delphic oracle. The ' Know thy Self of the Upanishads, means, know thy true Self, that \vhich underlies thine Ego, and find it and 318 LECTURE VII.
know it in the highest, the eternal Self, the One without a Second, which underlies the whole world.

This was the final solution of the search after the Infinite, the Invisible, the Unknown, the Divine, a search begun in the simplest hymns of the Veda, and ended in the Upanishads, or as they were after wards called the Vedanta, the end or the highest object of the Veda.
I can do no more than read you some extracts from these works, which stand unrivalled in the literature of India, nay, in the literature of the world.
Prajapati and Indra.

The first extract is from the TTMndogya Upanishad (VIII, 7-12). It is a story representing Indra, as the chief of the Devas or gods, and Viro&ana, as the chief of the Asuras, seeking instruction from Prar/apati. This, no doubt, sounds modern, if compared with the hymns of the Rig-Veda, yet it is anything but modern, if compared with all the rest of Indian literature. The opposition be tween Devas and Asuras is, no doubt, secondary, but traces of it begin to show themselves in the Rig- Veda, particularly in the last book. 'Asura,' living, was originally an epithet of certain powers of nature, particularly of the sky. In some passages one feels inclined to translate deva asuraA by the living gods. After a time asura is used as an epithet of certain evil spirits also, and at last it occurs in the plural as the name of the evil spirits, opposed to the Devas, the bright, kind, arid good spirits. In the Brahmawas that distinction is firmly established, and nearly everything is settled there by battles between Devas and Asuras.

PHILOSOPHY AND RELIGION. 319 That Indra should represent the Devas is natural. Viro£ana, however, is of later date : the name does not occur in the hymns. He appears first in the Taittiriya Brahmana, I, 5, 9, i, where he is introduced as the son of Prahrada and Kayadhu. Pra(/apati has assumed in this story his later character, as a kind of supreme god ; he is even represented as father of Indra in the Taittiriya Brahma??a, I, 5, 9, i.

The object of our legend is evidently to show the different stages by which we are to arrive at a knowledge of the true Self in man. Pra^apati speaks at first in an equivocal way, saying that the person seen in the eye is the Self. He means the seer, as independent of the eye, but his pupils misunderstand him, the Asura supposing that the small body seen in the pupil of the eye as in a mirror, is the Self, the Deva imagining that the shadow or the image in the mirror or in the water is the Self. But while Viro^ana is satisfied, Indra is not, and he is then led on to seek the Self, first in the person who, freed from the impres sions of the senses, is dreaming ; then in the person who has ceased to dream and is quite unconscious. Dissatisfied, however, with this, which seems to him utter annihilation, Indra is at last allowed to see that the Self is he who uses the senses, but is distinct from them, the person, in fact, seen in the eye, i. e. perceived in the eye, as the seer; or again, he who knows that he is the knower, while the mind, the divine eye, as it is called, is but his instrument. We find here the highest expression of the truth as seen by the dwellers in the forest, the highest goal reached by them in their search after the infinite.

SEVENTH ' Pra^apati said : " The Self which is free from sin, free from old age, from death and grief, from hunger and thirst, which desires nothing but what it ought to desire, and imagines nothing but what it ought to imagine, that it is which we must search out, that it is which we must try to understand. He who has searched out that Self and understands it, obtains all worlds and all desires." i.
' The Devas (gods) and Asuras (demons) both heard these words, and said : " Well, let us search for that Self by which if one has searched it out, all worlds and all desires are obtained."

1 Thus saying Indra went from the Devas, Viro&ana from the Asuras, and both, without having com municated with each other, approached Pragdpati, holding fuel in their hands, as is the custom for pupils approaching their master.' 2.
' They dwelt there as pupils for thirty -two years. Then Pragrapati asked them : " For what purpose have you both dwelt here 1 "

' They replied : " A saying of yours is being repeate.d, viz. 'the Self which is free from sin, free from old age, from death and grief, from hunger, and thirst, which desires nothing but what it ought to desire, and imagines nothing but what it ought to imagine, that it is which we must search out, that it is which we must try to understand. He who has searched out that Self and understands it, obtains all worlds and all desires.' Now we both have dwelt here because we wish for that Self. " ' 3.
' Praj/iipati said to them : " The person that is PHILOSOPHY AND RELIGION. 321 seen in the eye \ that is the Self. This is what I have said. This is the immortal, the fearless, this is Brahman."
' They asked : " Sir, he who is perceived in the water, and he who is perceived in a mirror, who is he \ "
' He replied : " He himself alone is seen in all these2."' 4.
zh: 吠陀詩人不再頌揚蒼穹或黎明，他們不再歌頌因陀羅的勇武，或毘首羯磨與生主（Prajāpati）的智慧。正如他們自己所說，他們「彷彿被迷霧與空泛言辭所籠罩」¹。另一位詩人則說²：「我的耳朵消逝，我的眼睛消逝，連同棲息於我心中的光明；我那懷著遠方渴慕的心智離我而去；我該說什麼，又該想什麼？」
> 或者又說：「我自身一無所知，在此詢問那些知曉的智者；我雖無知，但願得以學習；那位設立六界者，是否即是以未生之存在（unborn Being³）形式存在的那個『一』？」
這些風暴預示著更晴朗的天空與新的春天。

> 梨俱吠陀，X，82，7，*nihareva pravṛttāḥ / kalpya kā asutṛpaḥ uktthāśāsaḥ caranti*。 在我們開始探究宗教學的性質與範疇之前，有必要先釐清一項根本區別——即宗教學與比較神學之間的區別。宗教學，正如我將在本講義中使用的術語，指的是一門純粹科學性的學科，它致力於客觀地研究宗教現象，如同語言科學研究語言現象一般。它不涉及任何神學預設，也不試圖為任何特定宗教辯護或攻擊。它的唯一目標是理解宗教的本質、起源與發展，並揭示其在不同民族與文化中的表現形式。 另一方面，比較神學則帶有更明確的神學意圖。它通常從某一特定宗教的立場出發，利用比較方法來闡明該宗教的優越性，或證明其教義的普遍有效性。比較神學無疑有其正當地位，但它與宗教學的區別在於：前者是神學的分支，後者則是純粹的科學。 為了說明這種區別，我們可以回顧語言科學的發展歷程。在比較語法學興起之前，人們對語言的思考往往帶有神學色彩——例如，試圖證明某種語言是原始語言，或某種語法結構反映了神聖的秩序。然而，一旦語言科學確立了其比較方法，它便不再服務於任何神學目的，而是專注於追溯語言變化的規律，重建原始形式，並解釋語言多樣性的成因。宗教學的目標正是如此：它要成為一門關於宗教的科學，而非某種宗教的科學。 因此，我們必須將宗教學與比較神學嚴格區分開來。宗教學不問哪一種宗教是「真」的，而是問：宗教如何產生？它經歷了哪些階段？它的基本觀念——如「無限者」、神靈、靈魂、儀式——是如何形成的？這些問題只能通過歷史與比較的方法來回答。 值得注意的是，宗教學的比較方法並非隨意比附。它要求研究者具備精確的語言學知識，能夠直接查閱原始文獻，並理解其文化脈絡。例如，當我們研究吠陀宗教時，我們不能僅憑翻譯文本就下結論，而必須深入梵文原文，體會那些詩句在特定儀式中的意義。同樣，當我們比較雅利安宗教與閃語族宗教時，我們必須考慮到兩者在語言結構、社會組織與歷史經驗上的差異。 最後，我們必須警惕一種常見的誤解：即認為宗教學試圖將所有宗教歸結為某種共同本質，或否認宗教之間的實質差異。恰恰相反，宗教學承認宗教現象的多樣性，並試圖解釋這種多樣性如何從共同的根源中分化出來。正如語言科學並不否認不同語言之間的差異，反而通過比較來揭示它們的親緣關係，宗教學也致力於在差異中發現規律，在多樣性中追溯統一性。 這便是宗教學的任務——一項既艱巨又崇高的任務。它要求我們放下偏見，以科學的冷靜與歷史的同情來審視人類最深刻的經驗。唯有如此，我們才能真正理解宗教的本質，並為比較神學提供堅實的基礎。
2 同上，VI, 9, 6：「願我的言語、我的氣息、我的眼目、這置於我心中的光明，都得以擴展；願我的心意與思慮遠離惡念；我將說些什麼？我又將如何思量？」
> 此處引文為梵文，依規則保留不譯。
終於¹，那「一」、那「自我」的存在被大膽地斷言：它自存自立，存於一切受造物之前，甚至遠在諸神之前，以至於連諸神本身也不知此創造從何而生。

> 「在萬物存在之前，」我們被告知，「在死亡或永生存在之前，在晝夜尚未區分之前，已有『那獨一者』。祂無息而自呼吸。除祂之外，此後無物存在。那時唯有黑暗，萬物初始皆隱於幽暗——一切如同無光之洋。隨後，那被外殼包裹的胚芽，即獨一者，由熱力所生。」詩人如此持續思索萬物起源的問題：獨一者如何成為眾多？未知者如何成為已知或得名？無限者如何成為有限？他最終以這些詩句收尾：「誰知曉這奧秘？誰在此宣說？這紛繁的創造從何、從何而生？諸神本身乃後來出現——誰知這偉大創造從何而來？祂，一切偉大創造所從出者——無論祂的意志創造了萬物，或緘默不語——那至高先知，居於最高天，祂知曉，或者，連祂也不知。」 這些觀念在《梨俱吠陀》的頌歌中僅如初現的黯淡星辰，隨著時間推移變得愈加繁多而璀璨，最終在所謂的《奧義書》——仍屬吠陀文獻的最後創作——中形成完美的星河。
屬於吠陀時期，但其影響力遠遠超越該時期範圍的內容。
奧義書的哲學。
您記得，在我們所謂的讚歌時代之後，緊接著便是梵書（Brāhmaṇa）時代——這些古老的散文作品旨在描述並闡明古代的祭祀儀式。
在《梵書》的末尾，我們通常會見到所謂的《森林書》（Āraṇyaka），一部為那些離家隱居於森林獨處之人所撰寫的著作。
在《森林書》的末尾，或融入其內文之中，我們發現了最古老的《奧義書》（Upanishads），字面意為「座席」或「弟子圍繞其師的集會」；而在這些《奧義書》中，匯集了吠陀時代所有宗教哲學的精華。
為了讓各位對這些《奧義書》中所匯聚的思想寶藏有所認識，我可以告訴各位，我原本的打算是將這整系列講座全部用來闡述《奧義書》的教義。我本可從中獲得充足的素材；然而如今，在僅剩的短暫時間裡，我只能為各位勾勒出它們最粗略的輪廓。
這些奧義書中並不存在可稱為哲學體系的東西。它們是——就該詞的真正意義而言——對真理的揣測，經常彼此矛盾，卻又全都趨向同一個方向。古老奧義書的主調是「認識你自己」，但其含義遠比德爾斐神諭的「γνῶθι σεαυτόν」更為深刻。奧義書的「認識你自己」，意指認識你真正的自我，那個潛藏在你自我之下的本體，並找到它與——
……在至高者、永恆的自我、無二的獨一者中認識它，此獨一者乃整個世界的基底。
這是追尋無限者、不可見者、未知者、神聖者之探索的最終解答——這場探索始於吠陀中最質樸的讚歌，終於奧義書（Upanishads），亦即後世所稱的吠檀多（Vedanta），即吠陀的終極或最高目標。
我只能為各位朗讀這些作品中的若干選段，這些作品在印度文學中——甚至在世界文學中——都是無與倫比的。
**普拉賈帕提與因陀羅** > 「在吠陀頌詩中，我們看到因陀羅（Indra）被呼喚為『眾生之主』（Prajapati）；而在另一處，普拉賈帕提（Prajapati）又被稱為『最強有力的因陀羅』。……這種混淆不僅見於名稱，更深入至諸神的本質與功能。」（穆勒，《宗教學導論》，1873年）

第一段引文出自《闡陀迦奧義書》（VIII, 7-12）。這是一則故事，描述提婆（Devas）或眾神之首因陀羅（Indra），以及阿修羅（Asuras）之首維羅闍那（Virocana），一同向生主（Prajāpati）求取教誨。這則故事若與《梨俱吠陀》的讚歌相比，聽起來無疑是晚出的；然而若與印度文學的其他部分相比，卻絕非晚近之作。提婆與阿修羅之間的對立，無疑是後起的，但其痕跡在《梨俱吠陀》中已開始顯現，特別是在末卷之中。「阿修羅」（Asura）一詞原指「活著的」，最初是某些自然力量（尤其是蒼穹）的修飾語。在某些段落中，人們不禁想將 *deva asuraḥ* 譯為「活著的神祇」。過了一段時間，「阿修羅」也被用作某些邪靈的修飾語，最終以複數形式出現，成為邪靈的名稱，與光明、仁慈、良善的提婆（Devas）相對立。在梵書（Brāhmaṇas）中，此一區別已牢固確立，幾乎所有事物都透過提婆與阿修羅之間的戰鬥來決定。

因陀羅（Indra）代表提婆（Devas）是自然的。然而，維羅遮那（Virocana）的時代較晚：這個名字並未出現在頌詩中。他首次出現於《泰帝利耶梵書》（Taittirīya Brāhmaṇa, I, 5, 9, 1），在那裡他被介紹為缽羅訶羅陀（Prahrada）與迦耶度（Kayadhu）之子。生主（Prajāpati）在此故事中已呈現其後期的性格，作為一種至高神；在《泰帝利耶梵書》（Taittirīya Brāhmaṇa, I, 5, 9, 1）中，他甚至被描繪成因陀羅之父。

我們這則傳說的目標，顯然是要展示抵達人類真實自我（true Self）之知識所必經的各個階段。生主（Prajāpati）起初以一種模稜兩可的方式發言，聲稱眼中所見之人即是自我（Self）。他意指「觀看者」（seer），此觀看者獨立於眼睛；但他的弟子們卻誤解了他——阿修羅（Asura）以為在瞳孔中如鏡中所見的那個微小形體即是自我，提婆（Deva）則想像鏡中或水中的倒影或影像即是自我。然而，當毗盧遮那（Virocana）感到滿足時，因陀羅（Indra）卻不然；於是他被引導去追尋自我：首先是在那脫離感官印象、正在做夢的人身上；然後是在那已停止做夢、完全無意識的人身上。然而，因陀羅對此仍不滿意，因為這在他看來無異於徹底的湮滅。最後，他被允許看見：自我乃是運用感官、卻又與感官有別的那一位——事實上，就是眼中所見之人，亦即作為觀看者而在眼中被感知的那一位；或者說，是知道自己為認知者（knower）的那一位，而心智（mind）——即所謂的「神聖之眼」（divine eye）——不過是他的工具。我們在此發現了森林隱居者（dwellers in the forest）所見真理的最高表達，也是他們在追尋無限者（the Infinite）的過程中所達到的最高目標。
> 缽羅闍波提（Prajāpati）說：「那無罪、無老、無死、無憂、無飢、無渴之『自我』（Self），唯求其所當求，唯想其所當想——此即吾人所當尋覓者，此即吾人所當了悟者。凡尋得此『自我』並了悟之者，得一切世界與一切願望。」
> 「提婆（Devas，眾神）與阿修羅（Asuras，眾魔）都聽聞了這些話語，說道：『好，讓我們去尋求那個自我（Self），一旦尋得此自我，一切世界與一切慾望便皆能獲得。』」
1.  如此說著，因陀羅（Indra）便離開諸提婆（Devas），維羅恰那（Virocana）則離開諸阿修羅（Asuras）；兩者彼此未曾溝通，各自手持薪柴（作為弟子謁見師長之慣例），走向生主（Prajāpati）。
「他們作為弟子在那裡住了三十二年。隨後，生主（Prajāpati）問他們：『你們兩人住在這裡是為了什麼目的？』」
他們回答：「您有一句話正在被傳誦，即『那無罪、無老、無死、無憂、無饑、無渴，只欲其所當欲，只思其所當思之自我，正是我們必須探求的，正是我們必須理解的。凡探求並理解此自我者，得一切世界與一切慾望。』如今我倆居於此，正是因為渴望此自我。」
> 補盧迦波提（Prajāpati）對他們說：「那在眼中所見之人，即是自我（Self）。此即我所言。此即不朽者、無畏者，此即梵（Brahman）。」
他們問道：「尊者，那在水中被感知者，與那在鏡中被感知者，究竟是誰？」
他回答：「在這些一切之中，唯獨祂自身被看見。」⁴
